items.text: Chapter Four
Scientific classification of Tuta absoluta (Taxonomy).
4.1 Introduction

Scientific classification is the system that biologists use to arrange organisms into hierarchical groups, starting with broad groups that include large numbers of organisms, then gradually narrowing down to genus and species.

Classification is not limited to giving a name to the insect, but rather clarifies its location among living organisms and its degree of relationship to other species. It also helps to understand their anatomical characteristics, life cycle, feeding behavior, and methods of diagnosing and combating them.

Tuta absoluta belongs to the arthropods, then to the insects, then to the order Lepidoptera, which includes moths and butterflies. It is placed within the family Gelechiidae, a group that includes small moths, many of whose larvae feed inside plant tissue.
The species is currently classified under the name:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
While the previous name remains:
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917)
It is the most famous name in the agricultural field and applied research.
4.2 Complete scientific classification

rows: Tuta absoluta, or tomato leaf and fruit miner

items.text: The classification scale can be represented briefly as follows:
Animalia↓Arthropoda↓Hexapoda↓Insecta↓Lepidoptera↓Gelechioidea↓Gelechiidae↓Gelechiinae↓Gnorimoschemini↓Phthorimaea↓Phthorimaea absoluta
4.3 A simplified explanation of the classification levels
4.3.1 Animal Kingdom
Kingdom: Animalia
Tuta absoluta belongs to the animal kingdom because it is a multicellular organism and is not autotrophic, that is, it cannot manufacture its own food as plants do.

The insect gets its food from other organisms. In the larval stage, it feeds on plant tissues, especially tomato tissues, while its body does not carry out photosynthesis or produce organic materials from water and carbon dioxide.
Tuta absoluta shares general characteristics with other animals, including:

items: The ability to move at one or more stages of the life cycle.
The presence of sensory and nervous systems.

items.text: The animal kingdom includes very diverse groups, such as mammals, birds, fish, molluscs, worms, and insects.
4.3.2 Phylum Arthropoda
Phylum: Arthropoda
The insect belongs to the phylum Arthropoda, one of the largest animal groups in terms of number of species.
Arthropods are characterized by several basic characteristics:

items: The presence of an external structure.
A body made up of rings or parts.
Development of specialized sensory and nervous systems.

items.text: Exoskeleton
The insect's body is covered by an exoskeleton that mainly contains chitin. This cover provides:
But the exoskeleton does not expand constantly, so the larva needs to molt several times in order to increase its body size.
Articulated limbs
Arthropods are named because they have legs and appendages made up of parts connected by joints, allowing precise movement.
The phylum Arthropoda includes:
Although all of these groups are arthropods, Tuta absoluta is an insect, not an arachnid, arachnid, or crustacean.
4.3.3 Under the phylum Hexapoda
Subphylum: Hexapoda
Hexapoda refers to animals that have three pairs of true legs, meaning six legs.
This characteristic appears clearly in the adult insect, where the six legs are located on the chest area.
The entire body of an insect consists of three main regions:
The chest holds:

items: Two pairs of wings in an adult insect.

items.text: As for the larvae, it has three pairs of true thoracic legs, in addition to fleshy abdominal legs that help it move. The ventral legs are not true articulated legs like the thoracic legs.
4.3.4 Insect class
Class: Insecta
Tuta absoluta belongs to the insect class.
Adult insects are generally characterized by the presence of:

items: A chest made of three rings.
An abdomen made up of several rings.
One or two pairs of wings in most adult species.

items.text: Insects differ from arachnids and spiders in the number of legs and body division. Adult insects have six legs, while adult mites and spiders usually have eight legs.
Tuta absoluta undergoes complete transformation during its life cycle, and goes through four distinct phases:

The form, behavior and function of the larva differ significantly from that of the adult insect. The larvae is the feeding and damage phase, while the adult insect represents the mating, propagation, and egg-laying phase.
4.4 Order Lepidoptera
Order: Lepidoptera
Tuta absoluta belongs to the order Lepidoptera, the order that includes moths and butterflies.
The name Lepidoptera consists of two Greek roots that stand for:
This is due to the insect's entire wings being covered with fine microscopic scales.
General characteristics of Lepidoptera
Insects belonging to this order are characterized by several characteristics, including:

items: The presence of two pairs of wings in adult insects.
Cover the wings and body with fine scales.
The presence of a worm-like larval stage.
The presence of a clear head in the larva.
The larvae have three pairs of thoracic legs.
Transforming into a pupa before the adult insect emerges.

items.text: Tuta absoluta belongs to the group of small moths, and is not a fly. This point clearly distinguishes them from tunneling flies of the genus Liriomyza, which belong to the order Diptera Diptera.
Why is it a moth and not a diurnal butterfly?
There are general differences between diurnal moths and butterflies, although both belong to the order Lepidoptera.
The Tuta absoluta moth is characterized by:

items: Not bright gray or brown colours.
They are more active in darkness or low light.
It has wings with long bristles.
It hides during the day among the leaves of plants.

items.text: Therefore, the expression “Tuta moth” is more accurately entomological than the expression “Tuta moth,” even though the two expressions are understandable in field use.
4.5 above Gelechioidea type
The family Tuta absoluta is located within the superfamily Gelechioidea.
This group includes a large number of small moths, many of which are referred to as the group of micromoths or:
Microlepidoptera
Calling them microscopic moths does not mean that they are microscopic organisms, but rather that adult insects are small compared to larger butterflies and moths.
Many species within this group are characterized by the following:

items: The presence of hairs on the edges of the wings.
Accurate diagnosis depends on microscopic characteristics.
Larvae feed inside plant tissues or protected parts.
Relative specialization in some plant families.

items.text: These characteristics explain why Tuta absoluta is difficult to identify based on size and color alone because several small moths may look similar in appearance.
4.6 Gelechiidae family
Tuta absoluta belongs to the family Gelechiidae.
It is a large family of small moths, and its members are sometimes called:
Gelechiid moths
The family includes multiple species associated with plants and agricultural crops.
General characteristics of the species
Adult insects in this family are often characterized by the following:

items: Hind wings with long hairy edges.
Gray or brown colors help with camouflage.
Nocturnal activity or activity in low-light periods.

items.text: As for the larvae, in many species they feed in protected places, such as:

items: Between leaves wrapped or tied with silk threads.

items.text: This pattern is clearly visible in Tuta absoluta; The larvae burrow into tomato leaves, and also attack the tops, stems, and fruits.
The agricultural importance of the species
The Gelechiidae species includes a number of important agricultural pests, such as:

items: Tuta absoluta, or tomato leaf and fruit miner.
Other species infect grains, leaves, seeds and fruits.

items.text: The presence of Tuta absoluta and the potato tuber moth within the same family explains some of the similarities between them, but it does not mean that they are the same species.
4.7 under Gelechiinae subfamily
Tuta absoluta falls within the subfamily Gelechiinae, one of the inbred groups of the subfamily Gelechiidae.
The subfamily level is used to divide large families into smaller groups, comprising genera and species that share a more specific set of anatomical and evolutionary characters.
Differences between subspecies are not easily apparent in field examination, but often depend on:

items: Characteristics of the head and antennae.

items.text: Therefore, identifying the infestation in the field may reach the genus or species level through symptoms, larvae, and traps, while accurate taxonomic confirmation requires a specialist or laboratory examination.
4.8 Tribe Gnorimoschemini
The insect belongs within the subfamily Gelechiinae to the tribe:
Gnorimoschemini
A tribe is a taxonomic order located between subfamily and genus.
This tribe includes a group of small moths whose larvae are associated with a number of plants, especially some species of the Solanaceae family and other herbaceous plants.
Within this tribe there are closely related races, including:
The convergence between these genera led to the classification of Tuta absoluta changing several times, as it was historically transferred between:

The taxonomic revision published in 2021 returned the species to the genus Phthorimaea, after morphological analysis showed its presence in an evolutionary group that includes Phthorimaea operculella.
4.9 Gender Phthorimaea
A genus is a group of closely related species, sharing more specific anatomical and evolutionary traits than they do at the species or tribe level.
The insect currently belongs to the genus:
Phthorimaea
It is a genus that includes small moths, a number of which are related to plants of the Solanaceae family.
The most famous agricultural species within this genus are:
Phthorimaea absoluta
Tomato leaf and fruit miner, known as Tuta absoluta.
Phthorimaea operculella
Potato tuber moth, which is an important pest that infects potato leaves and tubers in the field and warehouse.
The relationship between the two types

rows: A pheromone specific to the tuber moth

items.text: Both pests may be found in the same agricultural area, especially when there are tomatoes and potatoes, so it is not enough to rely on seeing a small moth to determine the type.
4.10 Type Phthorimaea absoluta
Species is the most precise major level of scientific classification used to identify an organism.
The full name of the species is:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
The first part refers to gender:
Phthorimaea
While the second part refers to the qualitative characteristic:
absoluta
absoluta is not written alone as a full scientific name because the correct name consists of the genus name and the specific character together.
This type is characterized by a comprehensive set of characteristics, including:

items: The external appearance of the egg, larva, pupa and moth.
Shape of body scales and wings.
Characteristics of male and female reproductive organs.
Strong association with tomato and Solanaceae families.

items.text: Scientific identification of the species does not depend on only one symptom, such as the presence of a tunnel in a leaf. Because several insects can form similar tunnels.
4.11 Why did the gender change from Tuta to Phthorimaea?
The type was known for many years as:
Tuta absoluta
However, its position within tribe Gnorimoschemini has remained a subject of taxonomic debate due to the similarity between several genera within this group.
Modern taxonomic revision has relied on the analysis of a set of comparable morphological characters between closely related genera.
The analysis showed that the species known as Tuta absoluta is in a monophyletic group that includes:
Phthorimaea operculella
It is the typical species of the genus Phthorimaea.
Based on this relationship, the species was returned to its original genus, and the name became:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
As for the genus Tuta, it remains a taxonomically valid genus, but it does not include this species according to the recent review.
The difference in the two names does not indicate a change in the insect or the nature of its damage, but rather a modification of its position within the classification system.
4.12 A simplified explanation of the idea of classification
Scientific classification can be likened to a graduated title that precisely identifies the location of an insect.
If a species represents an identifiable individual, the higher levels are something like:
The lower we go the definition becomes more specific.
For Tuta absoluta:

items: Animalia: tells us it's an animal.
Arthropoda: tells us it is an arthropod with an exoskeleton.
Hexapoda: tells us it is a hexapod.
Insecta: tells us it's an insect.
Lepidoptera: tells us it is a moth or butterfly with scaly wings.
Gelechiidae: places it among the small moths of this family.
Gnorimoschemini: links it to a finer group of closely related moths.
Phthorimaea absoluta: specifies the same type individually.

items.text: Thus, classification does not mean merely memorizing Latin names, but rather gradually clarifies the identity of the insect and its relationship to other organisms.
4.13 Taxonomic difference between Tuta and Leafminer
The classification helps clarify that Tuta absoluta is radically different from the tunneling flies known in the field as leaf miners.
The two species are similar in their ability to form tunnels inside leaves, but similarity in damage does not mean direct taxonomic affinity.
Tuta absoluta is taxonomically closer to the potato tuber moth than to the Liriomyza fly, although both may infest tomato leaves.
4.14 The taxonomic difference between the Tuta and the true weevil
Some farmers give the name “weevil” to Tuta, but the classification shows that this name is scientifically incorrect.
Tuta is a lepidopteran insect, whereas true weevils belong to the order Coleoptera.
4.15 The importance of classification in integrated management
Pheromone identification
Each species has a special pheromone composition, and the Tuta absoluta pheromone is not suitable for detecting all moths or leafminers.
Diagnosis of larvae
The Lepidopteran butterfly larvae differ from the Diptera fly larvae in:
Choose vital enemies
Some parasites and predators are associated with specific groups or stages, so species identification influences the selection of the biological control organism.
Pesticide resistance management
Sites of action and effective groups vary depending on the type of pest. Resistance registers and IRAC group rotation programs are also based on selecting the correct type.
Prevent confusion between traps
Tuta absoluta, potato tuber moth and other types of small moths may be found in the agricultural area.
Taxonomic identification prevents all moths in a trap from being interpreted as a single species.
Agricultural quarantine
The competent authorities use the scientific name and international symbol to identify the pest in:
Tuta Absoluta has the code EPPO:
GNORAB
It is a fixed code that allows the pest to be tracked even when its scientific name changes.
4.16 Classification and field diagnosis
Daily field inspection does not require memorization of all taxonomic ranks, but depends on understanding the group to which the pest belongs.
When diagnosing Tuta absoluta in the field, a set of indicators are examined:

items: The presence of waste inside the tunnels.
The shape of the caterpillar and its head.
The presence of thoracic and abdominal legs.
The presence of holes in the fruits.

items.text: The specialized taxonomic diagnosis may depend on:
Laboratory examination becomes necessary when the pest is discovered in a new area or when there are apparently similar species.
4.17 Chapter summary
Tuta absoluta belongs to the kingdom Animalia, phylum Arthropoda, and class Insecta. It is a moth of the order Lepidoptera, not a fly or a mite.
Its complete classification is as follows:
The full modern scientific name is:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
The name widely known in the agricultural field is:
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917)
The classification proves that:

items: A true weevil is a beetle of the order Coleoptera.
The potato tuber moth is a closely related species of Tuta within the genus Phthorimaea, but it is not the same species.

items.text: This classification allows precise identification of the pest, preventing confusion with other insects, and linking field diagnosis with appropriate means of monitoring, control, and agricultural quarantine.
